title: Mental Math Strategies for Addition
essential_question: What mental math strategies can you use to find sums?

pretest.question: Use mental math. 56 + 27 = ?

expected_errors.note: A: 56+20=76, then added 7-4=3 → 73; counting-on slip in ones phase

expected_errors.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 2 Lessons 4-6 — friendly numbers, break apart, multi-addend errors.
expected_errors.note: C: added 37 instead of 27 — miscounted 3 decades instead of 2
D: 56+26=82 — miscounted ones by 1

pretest.hint: Start at 56, add 2 tens, then add 7 ones.

pretest.question: On Monday, 46 boys and 38 girls bought lunch at school. How many students bought lunch?

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12-15 — multidigit addition strategy errors and partial-answer patterns.
expected_errors.note: B: 40+38=78, used wrong tens (subtracted instead of added); partial-answer or concept gap
C: 46+38=84 but student miscarried ones: 6+8=14, carried wrong → 74
D: 46+38=84 but student dropped the carried ten → 73

pretest.hint: Break apart tens and ones, then combine.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

learn.title: What Is Mental Math?
learn.content: Mental math means solving problems in your head — without paper, pencil, or a calculator. You use strategies that make numbers easier to work with. There are several strategies you can use for addition.

visual_data.description: A child thinking with a thought bubble showing 28+35, with arrows showing the number changing to 30+33

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Strategy 1: Count by Tens and Ones
learn.content: Start with one number. Count on by tens for the tens digit of the other number. Then count on by ones. Example: 34 + 25. Start at 34. Count by tens: 44, 54. Count by ones: 55, 56, 57, 58, 59. So 34 + 25 = 59.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Strategy 2: Friendly Numbers (Adjust)
learn.content: Change one number to a 'friendly' number (a number ending in 0). Add, then adjust. Example: 17 + 25. Change 17 to 20 (add 3). 20 + 25 = 45. Adjust: 45 - 3 = 42. So 17 + 25 = 42.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Strategy 3: Break Apart (Place Value)
learn.content: Break each number into hundreds, tens, and ones. Add each place separately. Example: 116 + 203. Hundreds: 100 + 200 = 300. Tens and ones: 16 + 3 = 19. Total: 300 + 19 = 319.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Strategy 4: Make a Ten
learn.content: Move a small amount from one number to the other to make a ten. Example: 28 + 35. Take 2 from 35 and give it to 28: 30 + 33 = 63. Or: add 2 to 28 to make 30, then 30 + 35 = 65, then subtract 2: 65 - 2 = 63.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Choosing the Best Strategy
learn.content: Different problems work best with different strategies. Count by tens and ones: good for small addends. Friendly numbers: good when one number is close to a ten. Break apart: good for 3-digit numbers. Make a ten: good when one number ends in 8 or 9.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.title: Mental Math with 3-Digit Numbers
learn.content: You can use mental math with bigger numbers too! Example: 376 + 427. Break apart: 300 + 400 = 700. 76 + 27 = 103. 700 + 103 = 803. So 376 + 427 = 803.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

learn.math_talk_prompt: You solved 376 + 427 by breaking apart. Could you also use friendly numbers? Which strategy is faster here, and why? How do you decide which mental math strategy to use?

learn.title: Key Idea Summary
learn.content: Mental math strategies for addition: 1) Count by tens and ones — jump along a number line. 2) Friendly numbers — change to a nearby ten, add, adjust back. 3) Break apart — add hundreds, tens, and ones separately. 4) Make a ten — shift amounts between numbers to create a ten. Choose the strategy that makes the problem easiest for you!

expected_errors.citation: Fuson et al. (1997) p.140-143 — place-value decomposition and compensation error patterns.
expected_errors.note: A: 43+20=63, added 2 instead of 12; counted only 2 tens then stopped → 65

expected_errors.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 2 Lessons 4-6 — friendly numbers, break apart, multi-addend errors.
expected_errors.note: C: 43+40+2=85; counted 4 decades for 32 instead of 3 → over-counted by 1 decade
D: 43+31=74 — miscounted ones

try.hint: Start at 43, add 3 tens, then 2 ones.

practice_r2.question: Which strategy is BEST for adding 198 + 245?

choices.C: Break apart into hundreds, tens, ones
choices.D: Just add with paper and pencil

expected_errors.citation: Smarter Balanced G3 Item Specs 2015 — strategy selection and efficiency items.
expected_errors.note: A: student chose count-by-tens for 198; impractical — 19 decade hops needed

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12-15 — multidigit addition strategy errors and partial-answer patterns.
expected_errors.note: C: break-apart works but is less efficient; missed that 198 is closest to 200
D: 'use pencil and paper' — violates the mental-math objective of the lesson

solution_steps: Friendly numbers is the easiest strategy here
practice_r2.hint: Look at 198 — what friendly number is it close to?

practice_r3.hint: Add the first two, then add the third using friendly numbers.

practice_r3.question: Jake solved 298 + 156 like this: '300 + 156 = 456, then 456 - 2 = 454.' What strategy did Jake use?

expected_errors.note: A: Jake used count-by-tens — incorrect; Jake changed the number to a friendly hundred

expected_errors.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 2 Lessons 4-6 — friendly numbers, break apart, multi-addend errors.
expected_errors.note: B: student said 'break apart' — Jake did not split the number into parts; he changed 298 to 300 and adjusted
D: 'make a ten' involves shifting ones between addends; Jake rounded to the nearest hundred

solution_steps: This is the friendly numbers (adjust) strategy
practice_r3.hint: What did Jake do to 298 before adding?

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

verification.math_note: 298+156: Jake used friendly numbers (298→300, +2 offset). ✓ (전략 식별 문항)

practice_r3.question: Lily solved 47 + 36 using friendly numbers: '50 + 36 = 86. Then 86 + 3 = 89.' What mistake did Lily make?

choices.A: She should have subtracted 3, not added 3
choices.C: She should have used break apart instead
choices.D: She didn't make a mistake — 89 is correct

expected_errors.note: B: 50+36=86 is correct — student mis-identified this step as wrong
C: friendly numbers strategy is valid; Lily's error is in the adjust step, not the strategy choice
D: 47+36=83, not 89 — student did not verify the actual answer

solution_steps: But she added 3 more instead of subtracting
Since she added 3 to make 50, she needs to subtract 3 from the answer
practice_r3.hint: If you add to make a friendly number, what do you do at the end?

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

verification.math_note: 47+36: Lily made 47→50 (+3), so must subtract 3: 86-3=83. ✓ (오류 분석 문항)

practice_r3.question: Marcus has 475 baseball cards. He buys 168 more at a shop and receives 97 as a gift. How many cards does he have now?

expected_errors.note: A: 475+168=643, 643+97=740 but student computed 643+87=730 — arithmetic slip

expected_errors.citation: Carpenter et al. (1998) p.12-15 — multidigit addition strategy errors and partial-answer patterns.
expected_errors.note: D: student added only 475+168=643 and stopped; omitted the final +97 step

practice_r3.hint: Add the first two, then use friendly numbers for +97.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies for Addition

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 4 — Mental Strategies: Friendly Numbers and Break Apart

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.2 Mental Computation Strategies

practice_r3.question: Show two different strategies to solve 199 + 364. Which gives the same answer?

choices.D: Both strategies always give different answers.

expected_errors.citation: Fuson et al. (1997) p.140-143 — place-value decomposition and compensation error patterns.
expected_errors.note: B: friendly method got 564 but forgot to subtract 1 → left answer as 564 (off by 1); or stated methods give different answers

expected_errors.citation: Fuson et al. (1997) p.140-143 — place-value decomposition and compensation error patterns.
expected_errors.note: C: friendly method: 200+364=564, then +1=565 instead of -1=563; wrong adjustment direction
D: 'strategies always give different answers' — violates the commutative/associative correctness principle

practice_r3.hint: Try both strategies carefully. Do you get the same number?

unit_intro_message: 이 단원에서는 받아올림 없이/있이 1000 이내 덧셈을 머릿속으로 빠르게 계산하는 전략들을 배웁니다. 수직선·십의자리 홉·친숙한 수·자리값 분리 전략을 익혀 어떤 문제에 어떤 전략이 가장 효율적인지 판단하는 눈을 기릅니다.
unit_close_message: 잘했어요! 이제 네 가지 암산 전략을 사용할 수 있게 되었습니다. 다음 레슨에서는 덧셈의 성질(교환법칙·결합법칙)을 활용해 더 빠른 전략을 탐구합니다.
